params.text:
Seeing the person, not the diagnosis

A diagnosis of dementia describes part of a person's health — it does not describe who they are. Behind every diagnosis is a whole life: a childhood, a career perhaps, relationships, achievements, losses and passions. Person-centred care means keeping that individual, not the condition, at the heart of everything you do.

When you know someone's story, care becomes easier and kinder. Knowing that a man was a lifelong gardener helps you understand why he wants to be outside. Knowing that a woman raised four children helps you see why she worries about “the little ones” in the afternoon. Their words make sense in the context of their life.

Using life history and identity

Life-history work is not just pleasant background — it is a practical tool for good care. Many services use a life-story book, a “this is me” document or a similar record. Read it, add to it, and use it.

  • Learn what matters: preferred name, routines, faith, food, music and past occupation.
  • Respect identity: support people to dress, groom and live in ways that feel like themselves.
  • Involve families: relatives hold precious knowledge and can help you understand the person.
  • Connect through interests: a shared love of football or a favourite hymn can open real communication.

When identity is honoured, people are calmer, more engaged and more themselves. Seeing the person behind the diagnosis is the foundation of everything that follows.

params.question: Why is knowing a person's life history useful when supporting someone living with dementia?

answers.text: It helps you understand the person's needs, wishes and behaviour, and connect with them meaningfully.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Life history is a practical tool that makes care more personal, more understandable and more effective.

answers.text: It lets you decide which stage of dementia the person has reached.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Assessing stages is not your role. Life history is about knowing the person, not judging their condition.

answers.text: It is interesting background but has little effect on day-to-day care.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Life history has a real, practical impact — it helps you understand behaviour and communicate well.

answers.text: It allows you to correct the person whenever their memories are inaccurate.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The aim is to connect and reassure, not to correct. Life history helps you understand, not to challenge someone's reality.
